Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First, the problem says that each case_id groups events So, the first thing I need to do is count how many events each case_id has That means I need a GROUP BY on case_id to get the count per case

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So, maybe I should start by creating a subquery or a CTE that calculates the number of events per case Lets say I do something like SELECT case_id COUNT as event_count FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: This would give me a list of case_ids along with how many events each has Then, I need to filter out the cases where event_count greater than 6 So, in the next step, I need to get the case_ids that have event_count less than or equal to 6

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Wait, but the problem says to return the full set of events for the remaining cases So, after identifying which case_ids are valid those with 6 events or fewer I need to select all events from event_log where case_id is in that list

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: To do this in SQL, I can use a subquery in the WHERE clause For example SELECT FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT less than or equal to 6

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Wait, but the HAVING clause is used to filter groups after aggregation So in the subquery, we group by case_id count the events and then keep only those groups where the count is less than or equal to 6 Then, the outer query selects all events from the original table where the case_id is in that filtered list Yes, that makes sense Let me check if this works

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Another way to do it is using a CTE Maybe thats clearer WITH valid_cases AS SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT less than or equal to 6 SELECT e FROM event_log e JOIN valid_cases v ON e case_id equals v case_id

Name: Validation - IND
Text: This should also work Both approaches are valid The first one uses a subquery in the WHERE clause the second uses a CTE with a join

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: But in DuckDB, which is more efficient Well, the problem doesnt specify performance just correctness Either approach should be correct

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Now, in DuckDB, the syntax for HAVING is correct So the subquery with GROUP BY case_id and HAVING COUNT less than or equal to 6 will give the list of valid case_ids Then, the outer query selects all columns from event_log where case_id is in that list

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Wait, the problem says returns only the events from cases that have six or fewer events in total So the output should be the same columns as event_log but only for the valid cases Yes, so SELECT FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN subquery would return all columns for those cases Alternatively, using the CTE approach with a join would also return all columns from event_log

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Wait, but in some SQL dialects, using IN with a subquery that might have duplicates is okay, but since the subquery groups by case_id, each case_id appears once, so IN is fine Yes, because the subquery returns distinct case_ids since its grouped by case_id so the IN clause will work correctly

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
